nodes.title: Explore the repo structure
nodes.description: Before changing anything, look around. Open the repo on GitHub.com or in your file explorer and understand what each folder is for. apps/ is for projects, docs/ is for business knowledge, workflows/ is for processes, journal/ is for notes.
nodes.tryIt: Open your repo and click through every folder. Read each README.md file inside them.

nodes.title: Run /setup to configure your OS
nodes.description: The /setup command is a 5-question interview that personalizes your repo. It writes your name, project, goals, and communication preferences to config.json so your AI tools know who you are and how to help. No AI tool? Edit config.json directly on GitHub.com.
nodes.tryIt: Open the repo in your AI tool and type /setup. Or edit config.json on GitHub.com -- fill in every field.

nodes.title: Build your operating manual
nodes.description: The /build-manual command asks 6 questions about your business and generates docs/operating-manual.md. This document serves double duty: it's a real business reference for humans, and it's the context layer that makes every AI tool connected to your repo smarter. From this point on, AI knows what you do, how you work, and where you need help.
nodes.tryIt: Run /build-manual in your AI tool. Answer the 6 questions about your business. Review the generated doc and edit anything that doesn't sound right.

nodes.title: Make your first commit
nodes.description: After running /setup and /build-manual, your repo has real content. Committing saves those changes with a description of what you did and why. This is the core rhythm: change something, describe the change, save it. Every commit is a point in time you can return to.
nodes.tryIt: Commit your config.json and operating manual with a message like: 'set up my OS and document how my business works'

nodes.title: Try a slash command
nodes.description: Slash commands are quick actions your AI follows. Try /plan with your 30-day goal. The AI reads your config.json and operating manual, so it already has full context about your business. This is the payoff: you documented your business, and now AI is genuinely useful.
nodes.tryIt: Type: /plan [your 30-day goal from config.json]. Then try: /brainstorm ideas to solve [the pain point from your operating manual]

tracks.title: Start Operating
tracks.description: Use your OS for real work

nodes.title: Track a task with GitHub Issues
nodes.description: GitHub Issues is your built-in task tracker. Each issue is a task, idea, or question with its own discussion thread. Your repo comes with templates for ideas, tasks, and questions so you don't start from a blank page.
nodes.tryIt: Go to your repo's Issues tab. Click 'New issue', pick the 'Task' template, and create an issue for something you need to do this week.

nodes.title: Write a journal entry
nodes.description: The journal/ folder is your thinking space. Write daily notes, weekly reflections, ideas, lessons learned. The value builds over time: searchable, versioned, always accessible. Your AI tools can also read it for context on what you've been thinking about.
nodes.tryIt: Create journal/week-1.md. Write 3-5 sentences about what you're working on this week and what you want to figure out.

nodes.title: Log a decision
nodes.description: Six months from now you'll ask 'why did I do it this way?' The decision log answers that. Copy the template in docs/decisions/, fill it out for a real decision you've made recently. Context, decision, reasoning, consequences.
nodes.tryIt: Copy docs/decisions/000-template.md to docs/decisions/001-chose-github-os.md. Fill it out: why did you decide to use GitHub OS?

nodes.title: Document a process you repeat
nodes.description: If you do something more than twice, write it down. SOPs go in docs/sops/. Could be how you onboard a client, publish content, handle invoices, or run a weekly review. Written in markdown, versioned automatically, readable by AI.
nodes.tryIt: Ask your AI tool: 'Help me write an SOP for [something I do regularly]. Save it to docs/sops/.' Review the output, edit it, commit.

nodes.title: Get feedback with /review
nodes.description: The /review command gives you structured feedback on anything: a document, a plan, a piece of writing. It returns 3 strengths, 3 improvements, and a suggested next step. Use it before you share or ship anything.
nodes.tryIt: Pick something you've written in your repo and run: /review [filename]. Try it on your operating manual or your first SOP.
